summary: Guest author Allison Pacelli, a mathematics professor at Williams College, reflects on her connection with Cathy O'Neil and promotes her PZ Math Calculus Prep Camp. She argues that math is genuinely difficult for everyone eventually, yet society wrongly accepts mathematical illiteracy. Pacelli contends that math education too often focuses on memorization rather than understanding, and that her camp addresses this by teaching the conceptual foundations of calculus. The post emphasizes that mathematical thinking builds transferable skills like logical reasoning, precision, and perseverance that benefit students across all fields.
key_points: Math is inherently difficult for everyone at some point, regardless of intelligence or past success, yet society uniquely tolerates people claiming they're 'not a math person'
Mathematical thinking improves general problem-solving, logical reasoning, and even writing skills across all disciplines
Traditional math education fails students by emphasizing memorization of formulas rather than genuine conceptual understanding
A week-long calculus prep camp can give students the conceptual foundation and confidence they need to succeed in formal calculus courses
With the right teacher or materials, everyone can enjoy and succeed at mathematics
money_quotes: Math is hard. Very hard! No matter how intelligent someone is or how amazing their past accomplishments, math eventually becomes difficult for anyone.
It's perfectly acceptable for someone in society to say, 'oh I'm not a math person.'
Math makes you smarter. It makes you a better problem solver. It makes you better at persevering and trying new ideas until you find a solution.
Unfortunately, far too often, math is taught in schools as a bunch of formulas to memorize and rules to use.
When I first took Calculus in high school, I did very well and got a 5 on the AP exam. But I didn't truly understand it.
Mathematical thinking also requires that we be very precise and clear. This precision and clarity translates into better arguments in any paper or debate.
key_insight: True mathematical understanding, rather than formula memorization, builds transferable thinking skills that benefit students across all areas of life.
